questions.question: Derive the relationship between focal length and radius of curvature for a spherical mirror.
questions.answer: Using geometry of a concave mirror, for a ray parallel to principal axis, the reflected ray passes through focus F. By triangle similarity and small aperture approximation, the distance from pole to focus f = R/2, where R is radius of curvature.

questions.question: An object is placed at 30 cm in front of a concave mirror of focal length 20 cm. Find the position, nature and size of the image.
questions.answer: Using mirror formula: 1/v + 1/u = 1/f → 1/v = 1/(-20) - 1/(-30) = -1/60 → v = -60 cm. Real, inverted, magnified (m = -v/u = 2).

questions.question: Why does a convex mirror never form a real image?
questions.answer: Convex mirror always diverges incident rays. The reflected rays appear to come from a point behind the mirror, so image is virtual irrespective of object position.

questions.question: Explain why the refractive index of a medium is always greater than 1.
questions.answer: Speed of light in any medium is less than in vacuum (v < c). Since n = c/v, refractive index is always greater than 1 for all material media.

questions.question: A light ray enters from medium A (n=1.5) to medium B (n=1.33) at 30°. Calculate the angle of refraction.
questions.answer: Using Snell's law: n₁ sin i = n₂ sin r → 1.5 × sin 30° = 1.33 × sin r → sin r = (1.5 × 0.5)/1.33 ≈ 0.564 → r ≈ 34.4°.

questions.question: Explain why a swimming pool appears shallower than its actual depth.
questions.answer: Light rays from the bottom bend away from normal when emerging into air (rarer medium), so rays appear to come from a higher point → apparent depth = actual depth / refractive index.

questions.question: Derive the formula for lateral displacement in a glass slab.
questions.answer: Lateral displacement d = t × sin(i - r) / cos r, where t = thickness, i = incidence angle, r = refraction angle (from Snell's law). Emergent ray parallel to incident ray but shifted sideways.

questions.question: A convex lens forms a real image three times the size of the object. If object is 24 cm from lens, find focal length.
questions.answer: m = -3 (real inverted), m = v/u → v = -3u = -72 cm (u = -24 cm). Lens formula: 1/f = 1/v - 1/u → 1/f = -1/72 + 1/24 = 1/36 → f = 36 cm.

questions.question: Why does total internal reflection not occur when light travels from rarer to denser medium?
questions.answer: When going from rarer to denser, light always bends towards normal and refracts out; critical angle condition (sin c = 1/n) only applies from denser to rarer medium.

questions.question: Calculate the critical angle for diamond (n = 2.42) in air.
questions.answer: Critical angle c = sin⁻¹(1/n) = sin⁻¹(1/2.42) ≈ sin⁻¹(0.413) ≈ 24.4°.

questions.question: Explain the formation of mirage in deserts using total internal reflection.
questions.answer: Hot air near sand is less dense → light from sky bends upwards due to gradual refraction, then TIR occurs at steeper angles, creating illusion of water (inverted sky image).

questions.question: Why do we observe advance sunrise and delayed sunset?
questions.answer: Atmospheric refraction bends sunlight towards Earth even when Sun is below horizon → apparent position higher, causing Sun to appear 2 minutes earlier at sunrise and later at sunset.

questions.question: Two thin lenses of power +4 D and -2 D are kept in contact. Find the equivalent power and focal length.
questions.answer: P = P₁ + P₂ = 4 - 2 = +2 D. Equivalent focal length f = 1/P = 1/2 = 0.5 m = 50 cm.

questions.question: An object is placed 15 cm in front of a convex lens of focal length 10 cm. Find nature, position and magnification of image.
questions.answer: 1/v - 1/u = 1/f → 1/v = 1/10 + 1/(-15) = 1/30 → v = +30 cm. Real, inverted, m = v/u = -2 (twice magnified).

questions.question: Explain why dispersion occurs during refraction but not during reflection.
questions.answer: Refraction depends on speed which varies with wavelength (different colours bend differently). Reflection depends only on angle and is independent of wavelength → no dispersion.
